references.notes: This verse refers to the concept of *witr* (blood-vengeance), which was a significant quasi-ritual duty in pre-Islamic Arabian society, tied to honor and the deceased.
This phrase refers to 'days of severe ill-fortune' or 'inauspicious days,' reflecting a pre-Islamic belief in specific times or cosmic forces bringing bad luck or fate.

The 'days of ʿĀd' refer to the legendary destruction of the ancient people of ʿĀd, a common pre-Islamic trope illustrating divine retribution or the inexorable force of fate (dahr) against the arrogant.
This verse contains a hypothetical reference to the dead rising from their graves, which reflects a pre-Islamic conceptual understanding of death and the afterlife.
The phrase 'if the graves were opened' implies a pre-Islamic belief in the possibility of the dead returning or communicating, touching on the nature of death and the afterlife.
The tribal name 'Taym al-Lāt' explicitly names al-Lāt, a prominent pre-Islamic Arabian goddess.
A 'manṣaba' (standing stone or altar) where pots are placed is a clear reference to a pre-Islamic ritual space or sacrificial practice.
The tribal name "Taym al-Lāt" explicitly refers to the pre-Islamic goddess al-Lāt, indicating her worship.
The term "al-manāyā" refers to fates or deaths, often conceived as an impersonal cosmological force in pre-Islamic belief.
The explicit mention of swearing a solemn oath ("ḥalaftu yamīnan") reflects a pre-Islamic ritual practice where oaths were binding and often implicitly or explicitly invoked divine witness.
This verse describes women tearing their clothes (شقّاً), which is a pre-Islamic mourning ritual.
The women are compared to "al-Sa'ali," which are female jinn or ghoulish creatures in pre-Islamic Arabian folklore.
The horses are compared to "al-Sa'ali," which are female jinn or ghoulish creatures in pre-Islamic Arabian folklore.
This verse describes professional mourners (al-bawākī) weeping and wailing (al-ʿawīl) for Kulayb, which are explicit pre-Islamic mortuary practices.

references.entity_or_term: وَرَبِّ الراقِصاتِ إِلى مِنّي, وَرَبِّ الحِلِّ وَالإِحرامِ
references.notes: This verse contains two oaths invoking a 'Lord' (رَبّ) associated with pre-Islamic pilgrimage rituals, specifically the journey to Minā and the sacred states of ḥill and iḥrām.
The verse describes women lamenting and mourning, which is a common pre-Islamic mortuary practice.
The verse mentions 'تَيمَ اللاتِ' (Taym al-Lāt), which is a tribal name explicitly incorporating the pre-Islamic goddess al-Lāt.

The phrase 'حَلَفتُ يَميناً' (I swore an oath) refers to the solemn act of oath-taking, a significant ritual practice in pre-Islamic Arabian religion, often implicitly or explicitly invoking divine witness.
This verse contains an oath sworn by 'the Lord of the dancing she-camels,' which implies a pre-Islamic deity or sacred entity associated with ritualistic camel processions.
The terms 'ṭulla' and 'yuṭlal' refer to blood going unavenged, which is a direct reference to the pre-Islamic concept of blood vengeance (tha'r), a crucial duty related to death and honor.
